replies.text:
To be honest, I had an idea for how a remake should be. Please feel free to criticize me if you don't like it.
This story starts a bit further in the timeline of the actual snow White.
After the poison apple plan completely failed, the evil witch or whatever would look at the mirror and ask how to fix all this and make the prince by her side or whatever. The mirror would give her a hint about some location or item hidden in a ruin which had the absolute power of turning the time back. The witch would manage to succeed in turning back time and return to the time when she was about to send the assassin off to snow White to finish her off. She still calls the huntsman to a private room and finishes him off as he was a massive hurdle for her in her past life. She the gets a very great idea of making the prince to her side. She calls over the prince to her kingdom and rules the kingdom for a while with showing kindness to the citizens just to show the prince off as just how good she is. She then invites the prince off to a dinner and talks about an alliance. The alliance succeeds and the prince returns to his home kingdom never to return (probably). Then witch then sets off herself to kill snow White with her vary own hands. All this while the lead, snow White was inside the cottage with her dwarf friends. The dwarfs, being magical beings had realized that something was wrong with the world. Occasionally checking the corset lacing of snow White and warning her from touching combs and apples. Snow White also started to feel distant from the dwarfs due to their strange behavior. And when the witch arrives as usual to slay snow White, not as a young lady, but as her own with a magical wand. Due to the slightly altered timeline, dwarfs were there to protect snow White. They manage to make snow White escape all danger and go out through a back door. The witch ignores the dwarfs and runs after snow White and the prince arrives in the middle. All this time the prince was slowly getting his memories back and arrives just in time, the witch fires the accurate should and the prince comes in the middle and collapses. Since the witch was out of magic she runs away. Leaving the crying snow White and the dead prince in her arms. The dwarfs come in time to console her. The dwarfs had also regained the me by now and they tell snow White everything about the change of events and the artifact. Snow White gets up and decided to fix the time line. She infiltrates the castle with the help of the dwarfs and gets to the front doors of the witch queen. But the entrance had a guard to block them. The guard asks them the reason for coming there. Snow White tells the guard everything. The guard, who also happened to be a very good friend of the hunter that the witch queen killed, allows snow White and the dwarfs to enter the room. Snow white enter and soon finds a locked chest. The dwarfs smash open the chest but that causes the witch who was just sleeping next room to wake up. Snow White runs away with the dwarfs and the artifacts and the witch kills the guards for helping snow White. She then chases snow White and the seven dwarfs continuously to the ruins and manages to kill the seven dwarfs with her magic on the way. Snow White, believing the dwarfs words, runs straight for the ruins and places the artifact in the right position. The sun shines brightly and the voice of the world comes up.
The voice describes how the witch interfered with the real timeline and changed the history. That's why it punishes her and completely removes her existence from the world and as a reward for snow White it resets the world and in this reset, it was a World where snow White's mother didn't die and snow White lives a happy life of a princess and marries the prince in the future. And one day, even though she doesn't remember anything that happened in the future and about the evil witch. She one day visits the dwarfs accidentally. The dwarfs, remembering everything, whole hearted invites snow White into their house... The end.

I know this may not be perfect and may not suit everyone's mood. So please comment back if you want any changes!

It was a pain in the *ss though because I wrote it all in 3 am.

author: @vaishnavigopinath8643
text:
One of the problems lies in the fact that in the original, Snow White was known for her childish nature, kindness and passion. But this also made her an easy target for deceit and those who wished to do her harm, which made her more vulnerable to the Evil Queens lie regarding the apple. And the Evil Queen didnt know about the Prince, so she didnt believe Snow White would be saved. This made the apple scene more believable. The kind princess saw an old woman in need. The Evil Queen took advantage of Snow White's kindness.

In the Live action, Snow White is portrayed as more mature, empowering and independent. And the love interest was revealed to the Evil Queen within the first part. This makes the apple scene confusing and out of character. This Snow White knows the Evil queen is trying to kill her, so why would she follow the classic 'opening the door to the stranger hag'? Why would she eat an apple given to her by a stranger who claims to be an ally? Also, if the queen knew of her love interest,  why go through the trouble of the apple? It feels as though the movie is flickering between an original reflection and a modern retelling

The original Snow White may get backlash for her naviety, but one needs to remember that she's still a teenager under the thumb of her cruel and jealous stepmother. Of course dhe would be dreaming and wishing for her true love. There's  nothing wrong with wanting that. Despite her struggles, she still continued to be kind and smile.

replies.text:
I was in Sydney recently. I know that everybody knows about Sydney, Australia, but to give some extra information, it's the most populous city in the country (well over five million people). I was waiting to go into a movie. _Snow Woke_ was playing before my movie. Hardly anybody came out of the cinema, maybe a dozen people. This was on a Friday night in the best cinema in town. The weather was quite fine, so it's not as if people were staying at home, and about _a dozen people_ come out of the thing. I think the screening of _The Brutalist_ in the same cinema earlier in the month had more people (with far less hype and about one twentieth of the budget).

Fun fact: the cinema in which I made this observation was opened in 1937. What year did _Snow White and the Seven Dwarves_ open? Answer: 1937. There's some poetic justice to that.

replies.author: @daddraws
replies.text:
@ Sure. Your comment shows how bias slips in unnoticed - like assuming only “normal” actors were hired or that CGI is better than casting real people. That mindset quietly pushes marginalized folks out. And while “little people” is used by some, others find it infantilizing - so even our language reflects how complex real inclusion is. It’s a reminder that progress isn’t just policy - it’s mindset, too. 

Setting that aside, Disney’s in a tough spot - they’ve tried to correct past missteps and face heat from both sides. Conservatives say they’re erasing tradition, while progressives argue they’re not doing enough. But honestly, they’re one of the few big players actually trying to evolve. Imperfect, sure - but they keep pushing forward despite years of backlash, and that effort counts for something. It seems that only moderates actually appreciate it.

replies.author: @Lexous248
replies.text:
@ Thank you for your viewpoint, I understand where you're coming from. However, I never said that "normal" people or CGI dwarfs were better, I actually believe the opposite. I thought this was clear in my confusion of why Disney didn't cast people with dwarfism, but I'm happy to explain further. 

The original Snow White movie is arguably the most universally recognized role for this marginalized group. A modern remake would have allowed people with dwarfism (who usually are turned down in Hollywood for their appearance) an amazing opportunity to break into the scene and show that their acting skills are more than what they look like.

Language is a way for us to categorize the world around us... which humans love to do- put things in categories to understand and make sense of things. It's hard to keep up with what's politically correct, as within the past few years, saying "little people" was more politically correct than saying "dwarfs". I put "normal" in quotes to imply it is just a saying, not because that's what I believe or what I think they should be called, plus it was more concise than saying "people without dwarfism".

replies.text:
Completely agree! Especially when you consider the thousands of hours of work, and the level of perfection needed to accomplish the 1937 movie. People like Rachel Zegler judge the movie solely on its political correctness and social commentary. They don't seem to get the fact that it's a fairytale, not a crowd funded documentary made to expose the propaganda aimed at young women to keep them in the kitchen. 

Before things like tv, movies and the internet, there were fairytales- beautiful captivating stories filled with magic, suspense, adventure and happy endings; stories where the underdog won in the end. To compare the labor of love ( literally and figuratively) whicg was the 1937 film, and this, is like comparing a European cathedral built by  generations of craftsman over a span of 100 years with today's mega churches- identical auditoriums with recessed lighting.

author: @GreyWolfLeaderTW
text:
Some food for thought:
     Walt Disney himself was *NOT* a fan of remakes or sequels. He only made two remakes of any of his short films in his lifetime: one Mickey Mouse short (Orphan's Benefit (1934) & (1941)), one Silly Symphony (The Ugly Duckling (1931) & (1939)). And he *NEVER* even considered remaking any of his feature-length films, animated or live-action.
     While everyone associates Walt and his company with retellings of fairy tale and folk stories, they were never the sole focus for him or his creative story team.
     Of the first five Golden Age films of his studio, two were classic fairy tales (Snow White & Pinocchio), one was a wholly original story (Dumbo), one was an experimental animated symphony film recontextualizing classic music and setting it to original animated segments (Fantasia), and one was based in a relatively obscure then-new novel written by a novelist who got far more exposure for his original work through Walt's spotlight through the animated film he created based on the story than the novelist otherwise would have gotten (Bambi).
     This is a trend that holds relatively true throughout the catalog of films Walt led production on over the course of his nearly 50 year career as a visionary and pioneer of film animation.
     Compare that (let alone the lengthy catalog of films and body of men and women who followed in his footsteps, seeking to honor the institution and legacy he established and built, from The Aristocats all the way up to [at least] Winnie the Pooh (2011)) to the modern trademarked corporate machine led by Bob Iger which has the vision and creativity of a robotic factory line. With the exception of an okay film here or there, from Moana (led by the legendary Disney directorial duo of John Musker and Ron Clements) to Zootopia to Coco (a Pixar film greenlit before John Lassater was given the heave-ho by the intersectional crowd for unverified claims of harassing the help), the only creative idea in the modern bureaucratic blob labeling itself Disney is to either regurgitate what was made before, or spend tons of money buying (assimilating) someone else's film catalog (goodbye 20th Century Fox & Blue Sky Studios).
